evt-eridu-temple: Ruins of the ancient Sumerian ziggurat at Eridu, southern Iraq, with stamped bricks from King Ur-Nammu's construction
evt-inanna-worship: The goddess Inanna (Ishtar) depicted on an Akkadian cylinder seal, shown with wings and weapons as the goddess of love and war
evt-wheel-invention: Reconstructed four-wheeled ceremonial wagon from the Celtic period, Landesmuseum Württemberg
evt-cylinder-seal: Cylinder seal and its clay impression showing the king-priest feeding the sacred herd, Uruk period, c. 3200 BCE
evt-cuneiform-invention: Large trilingual cuneiform inscription of King Xerxes carved into rock near Van, eastern Turkey
evt-beer-brewing: Decorated beer jug with birds, fish, and geometric motifs, Tel Eton, 12th century BCE
evt-silk-road-precursor: Raw lapis lazuli stone with golden pyrite inclusions, sourced from Afghanistan
evt-elamite-rivalry: Map showing the extent of ancient Elam in relation to Mesopotamia
evt-gilgamesh-person: 19th-century engraving depicting the Chaos Monster and the Sun God, an Assyrian mythological scene
evt-royal-tombs-ur: Black-and-white photograph of the Royal Cemetery of Ur excavations
evt-early-dynastic-wars: The Stele of the Vultures in the Louvre Museum, commemorating a Sumerian military victory
evt-sumerian-trade-networks: Map of the ancient Near East in the 3rd millennium BCE, showing Sumerian maritime trade routes and partner cities
evt-eannatum: Fragment of the Stele of the Vultures showing King Eannatum of Lagash leading troops, Louvre Museum
evt-shekel-weight: Electrum stater coin from Carthage, c. 310–290 BCE, showing the head of Tanit
evt-sumerian-astronomy: Babylonian clay tablet recording an appearance of Halley's comet in 164 BCE, British Museum
evt-lugalzagesi: Akkadian victory stele from the era of Lugal-zage-si, showing a prisoner exiting a cage, c. 2300 BCE, Louvre Museum
evt-sargon-akkad: Bronze head believed to represent an Akkadian king, possibly Sargon or Naram-Sin, Iraq Museum, Baghdad
evt-enheduanna: Calcite disk depicting Enheduanna, daughter of Sargon of Akkad, history's first known author
evt-naram-sin: Victory Stele of Naram-Sin showing the Akkadian king ascending a mountain over fallen enemies, Louvre Museum
evt-gudea-lagash: Diorite statue of Gudea of Lagash, dedicated to the god Ningishzida, c. 2120 BCE
evt-ur-nammu: Stone carving depicting King Ur-Nammu of the Third Dynasty of Ur
evt-code-ur-nammu: Clay tablet fragment bearing the Code of Ur-Nammu, the oldest surviving law code, Istanbul Archaeological Museum
evt-ziggurat-ur: Remains of the Great Ziggurat of Ur in southern Iraq, originally built c. 2100 BCE
evt-epic-gilgamesh-written: The Flood Tablet (Tablet 11 of the Epic of Gilgamesh), Neo-Assyrian clay tablet from the Library of Ashurbanipal, British Museum
evt-sumerian-king-list: The Weld-Blundell Prism, a four-sided clay prism inscribed with the Sumerian King List
evt-shulgi: Line drawing of a cylinder seal impression attributed to King Shulgi of Ur
evt-mari-kingdom: Ruins of a ziggurat near the royal palace at Mari, Syria
evt-babylonian-mathematics: Clay tablet YBC 7289 from the Yale Babylonian Collection showing a square with diagonal and sexagesimal calculations
evt-plimpton-322: Plimpton 322, a Babylonian clay tablet listing Pythagorean triples, c. 1800 BCE
evt-hammurabi: Votive monument dedicated to Hammurabi, king of Babylon, British Museum
evt-code-hammurabi: Upper portion of the Code of Hammurabi stele showing the king receiving authority from the god Shamash, Louvre Museum
evt-babylon-marduk: 19th-century engraving of an Assyrian relief depicting a battle between a chaos monster and a sun god
evt-atra-hasis: 19th-century engraving of an Assyrian mythological scene, used to illustrate Mesopotamian flood narratives
evt-hittite-sack-babylon: "The Fall of Babylon" — mezzotint by John Martin, 1831, depicting Cyrus the Great defeating the Chaldean army
evt-mitanni-kingdom: Map of the Mitanni kingdom at its greatest extent, showing major cities and archaeological sites
evt-amarna-letters: Five Amarna Letters on display at the British Museum — diplomatic correspondence between Egyptian pharaohs and Near Eastern rulers
evt-tukulti-ninurta: Stone base with cuneiform inscription showing Tukulti-Ninurta I standing and kneeling before a divine symbol, 13th century BCE
evt-assyrian-conquest-babylon: The Statue of Marduk depicted on a 9th-century BCE cylinder seal — Assyrian conquerors carried this sacred statue from Babylon as a trophy of war
evt-bronze-age-collapse: Map showing migrations, invasions, and destructions during the Bronze Age Collapse, c. 1200 BCE
evt-nebuchadnezzar-i: Kudurru (boundary stone) from the reign of Nebuchadnezzar I, recording a land grant and tax exemption, 12th century BCE
evt-tiglath-pileser-i: Stone relief depicting Tiglath-Pileser I, king of Assyria
evt-enuma-elish: 19th-century engraving of an Assyrian relief depicting the primordial battle of creation from Mesopotamian mythology
evt-mul-apin: The MUL.APIN tablet — clay tablet with two columns of astronomical text listing star catalogues, British Museum
evt-new-year-festival: Modern Assyrian community celebrating the Akitu (New Year) festival, 2019
evt-ashurnasirpal-ii: Stone relief showing King Ashurnasirpal II meeting a high official during a review of soldiers and prisoners
evt-nimrud: Pair of lamassu (winged human-headed bulls) guarding the entrance to Ashurnasirpal's palace at Nimrud
evt-shalmaneser-iii: Stone relief detail depicting King Shalmaneser III of Assyria
evt-battle-qarqar: The Kurkh Stela of Shalmaneser III, recording the Battle of Qarqar in 853 BCE
evt-babylonian-eclipse: Babylonian clay tablet recording astronomical observations, British Museum
evt-tiglath-pileser-iii: Gypsum wall panel relief of King Tiglath-Pileser III wearing royal robes with a full beard and ceremonial mace
evt-tiglath-pileser-conquests: Map showing Assyrian deportation routes — Tiglath-Pileser III systematized mass population transfers as imperial policy
evt-fall-of-israel: Panel from the Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III showing Israelite delegates bearing tribute to the Assyrian king, British Museum
evt-sennacherib: Cast of a rock relief of King Sennacherib from the mountain of Cudi Dağı, near Cizre, Turkey
evt-sennacherib-jerusalem: Remnants of the Broad Wall in Jerusalem, built by King Hezekiah in preparation for Sennacherib's siege
evt-babylonian-zodiac: 19th-century engraving of an Assyrian astronomical scene related to celestial observation
evt-nineveh-capital: The reconstructed Mashki Gate at the ancient site of Nineveh, Iraq
evt-esarhaddon: Basalt victory stele of King Esarhaddon holding ropes attached to conquered rulers, Pergamon Museum, Berlin
evt-assyrian-conquest-egypt: Map of the Neo-Assyrian Empire at its greatest territorial extent, 824–671 BCE
evt-ashurbanipal: Stone relief of King Ashurbanipal hunting a lion from horseback, British Museum
evt-library-nineveh: Cuneiform tablets from the Library of Ashurbanipal on display at the British Museum
evt-destruction-elam: Assyrian relief depicting the destruction of the Elamite city of Hamanu, c. 645–635 BCE
evt-battle-carchemish: Illustration of the Battle of Carchemish from Hutchinson's History of the Nations, 1915
evt-nebuchadnezzar-ii: Artist's reconstruction of the Etemenanki ziggurat in Babylon during the reign of Nebuchadnezzar II
evt-nebuchadnezzar-ii-person: Artist's reconstruction of the Etemenanki ziggurat in Babylon during the reign of Nebuchadnezzar II
evt-hanging-gardens: 19th-century artist's imagining of the Hanging Gardens of Babylon, inspired by early archaeological excavations
evt-chaldean-dynasty: Map of Babylonia during the Chaldean period, showing the centers of astronomical observation and major cities
evt-etemenanki: Architectural reconstruction drawing of the Etemenanki ziggurat in Babylon
evt-mesopotamia-destruction-jerusalem: 19th-century Bible illustration depicting the destruction of Jerusalem by Babylonian forces
evt-ishtar-gate: Reconstructed Ishtar Gate of Babylon, covered in blue-glazed bricks with relief figures of bulls and dragons, Pergamon Museum, Berlin
evt-nabonidus: Detail of a stele showing King Nabonidus in traditional Babylonian royal dress, holding symbols of authority
evt-fall-babylon-persia: "The Fall of Babylon" — mezzotint by John Martin, 1831, depicting the Persian conquest
evt-cyrus-cylinder: The Cyrus Cylinder, a clay barrel inscribed with a declaration by Cyrus the Great after his conquest of Babylon, British Museum
evt-mehrgarh-settlement: Archaeological ruins of Mehrgarh, one of the earliest Neolithic farming villages in South Asia, Balochistan, Pakistan
evt-ravi-phase-harappa: Map of early Harappan sites during the Ravi and Hakra phases, showing the earliest settlements in the Indus region
evt-kot-diji-founded: View of Kot Diji Fort from the town below, Sindh, Pakistan
evt-kalibangan-plowed-field: The western mound (citadel) at the ruins of Kalibangan, Rajasthan, India
evt-early-indus-script: Clay impression of an Indus Valley seal showing five characters of the undeciphered Indus script
evt-kot-diji-burned: Kot Diji Fort, Sindh, Pakistan — the site shows evidence of a major fire destruction layer
evt-mature-harappan-begins: Map of the Indus Valley Civilization during the Mature Harappan phase, 2600–1900 BCE
evt-mohenjo-daro-built: View of the Buddhist-era stupa mound at Mohenjo-daro, with the ancient city ruins in the foreground
evt-dholavira-built: Layout plan of Dholavira showing the city's distinctive three-part division: citadel, middle town, and lower town
evt-rakhigarhi: Excavation site at Rakhigarhi, the largest known Harappan settlement, Haryana, India
evt-urban-planning: The excavated grid layout at Lothal — every major Harappan city was built to a rectilinear plan with streets running on cardinal directions.
evt-harappa-city: The mounds of Harappa in Pakistani Punjab — the northern anchor city, and the one British railway engineers famously mined for track ballast in 1856.
evt-drainage-system: Covered brick drains at Lothal — Harappan cities built drainage networks more sophisticated than anything in the ancient world until Rome, two thousand years later.
evt-standardized-weights: Cubical chert weights from Mohenjo-daro, now in the Ashmolean — ground to binary ratios (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32) and accurate to a tenth of a gram across the entire civilization.
evt-indus-script-seals: Collection of Indus script characters from various seals, showing the range of the still-undeciphered writing system
evt-granaries-harappa: Ruins of the granary and great hall at Harappa — centralized grain storage suggesting organized food distribution
evt-bead-making: Harappan carnelian beads recovered from the Royal Cemetery at Ur in Mesopotamia — the export trade in these beads reached 1,500 miles from their workshops at Chanhu-daro and Lothal.
evt-dancing-girl: The Dancing Girl of Mohenjo-daro, a bronze figurine c. 2300–1750 BCE, one of the most famous Indus Valley artifacts
evt-pashupati-seal: The Pashupati Seal from Mohenjo-daro, depicting a seated figure surrounded by animals, possibly a proto-Shiva deity
evt-mother-goddess: A Mature Harappan terracotta female figurine with elaborate headdress and jewelry — a figurine type found at every major Indus city, usually interpreted as a mother goddess.
evt-cotton-cultivation: A cotton plant in bloom — the Indus Valley was the earliest known center of cotton cultivation
evt-urban-wells: View of the mound at Mohenjo-daro, where public wells were integral to the city's water supply system
evt-harappan-city-councils: Map of the Indus Valley Civilization showing major governance centers during the Mature phase
evt-lothal-founded: The ancient dockyard at Lothal, Gujarat — the world's oldest known dock, connecting the Indus trade network to the Arabian Sea
evt-mesopotamia-trade: Map showing trade connections between the Indus Valley and Mesopotamia, with major routes and trading centers
evt-dilmun-trade: Cuneiform receipt for garments sent by boat to Dilmun (Bahrain), documenting the maritime trade that connected India and Mesopotamia, British Museum
evt-magan-copper-trade: Map of the 3rd millennium BCE Near East showing Magan (Oman), source of copper traded to the Indus Valley
evt-dholavira-signboard: The 'Ten Indus Scripts' signboard discovered near the northern gateway of the Dholavira citadel — the largest known Indus inscription
evt-dholavira-reservoirs: Aerial view of the excavated water reservoir system at Dholavira, Gujarat, India
evt-unicorn-seal: A steatite seal from Mohenjo-daro depicting the mysterious 'unicorn' — a one-horned bovine figure that appears on most Harappan seals, never explained.
evt-ivory-comb: A carved-ivory long die from Mohenjo-daro at the British Museum — one of the gaming pieces that hint at a sophisticated craft industry in worked ivory.
evt-administrative-seals: A group of steatite seals from Harappa at the National Museum, Delhi — pressed into wet clay to mark packages, doors, and administrative records across the civilization.
evt-fire-altars: The western citadel mound at Kalibangan, where fire altars suggesting ritual practices were discovered
evt-burial-practices: A Harappan burial at Rakhigarhi — most people were buried in simple pits with pottery grave goods, no pyramids, no royal tombs, no obvious social hierarchy in death.
evt-fortification-walls: The massive fortification walls at Dholavira in Gujarat — up to 60 feet thick at the base, enclosing a city that survived in the salt desert for nearly 1,200 years.
evt-ruler-measurement: Brick drainage channel at Lothal — the standardized brick sizes reflect the Indus decimal measurement system
evt-animal-veneration: An elephant seal from the Indus Valley at the British Museum — one of many animals that recur on Harappan seals alongside the bull, the tiger, the rhinoceros, and the buffalo.
evt-indus-metallurgy: The Dancing Girl of Mohenjo-daro — a 4.1-inch bronze figurine cast in lost-wax and dated to around 2500 BCE, the most famous surviving example of Harappan metallurgy.
evt-toy-carts: A Harappan terracotta toy cart — children's toys from Mohenjo-daro that also provide some of the earliest evidence for wheeled vehicles in South Asia.
evt-indus-games: Cubic dice from Mohenjo-daro in the Ashmolean — the Harappans were rolling six-sided dice with dots two thousand years before anyone else we know of.
evt-climate-shift: Global map showing regions affected by the 4.2 kiloyear climate event that contributed to the Indus decline
evt-surkotada: Map of Indus Valley Civilization sites, including Surkotada where controversial horse bone evidence was found
evt-decline-begins: Map of the Mature Harappan phase showing the full extent of the civilization before its decline began
evt-mohenjo-daro-massacre: The stupa mound at Mohenjo-daro — scattered skeletons found here sparked debate about the city's violent end
evt-cemetery-h: Map of Rigvedic-era geography showing the transition zone between late Harappan and early Vedic cultures
evt-deurbanization: Map of the Indus Valley Civilization showing the pattern of urban abandonment during de-urbanization
evt-pirak-settlement: Ruins near the Pirak archaeological site in Balochistan, a bridge settlement between Harappan and later cultures
evt-late-harappan-craft: Cemetery H pottery from a post-urban Harappan burial ground — the distinctive Late Harappan style that appeared as the great cities emptied out.
evt-vedic-period-begins: Map of Early Vedic culture, c. 1700–1100 BCE, showing the geographic shift from Indus centers to the Ganges plain
evt-end-civilization: Map of the full Mature Harappan phase — by 1300 BCE, all of these urban centers had been abandoned
evt-great-bath: The Great Bath at Mohenjo-daro — a 39-by-23-foot brick-lined tank on the citadel, sealed with bitumen against leaks and fed by its own well.
evt-priest-king: The 'Priest-King' of Mohenjo-daro — a 7.3-inch steatite bust with serene half-closed eyes that may not depict a priest, or a king, or any single person at all.
evt-chanhu-daro: Beads from Chanhu-daro in Sindh — a small Harappan city that specialized in bead and seal manufacturing for export across the civilization.
